The camera signature becomes a Netflix teaser drop.
Mechanisms: ritual-as-media, broadcast-hijack, celebrity-as-channel, serialised-teaser
Idea: During three matches at the Indian Wells tournament, Carlos Alcaraz, instead of signing, wrote cryptic messages on the cameras that drove conversation on TV. 'No me lo creo, no more excuses', 'TUZUH? Hoy 8…', 'No more secrets'. One last message that was accompanied by the revelation in out of home and social media: a new Netflix series is coming.
Insight: Sport broadcasts already have a ritual moment of personal messaging — the camera-lens signature — that nobody has used as a serialised content drop.

visual.style_description: Dark cinematic board with a tight portrait of Carlos Alcaraz pointing at the lens, the campaign title 'Alcaraz Signs' inked in red marker over his hand; press logos run across the top, results figures stack on the right, frame stills of the broadcast hijack and out-of-home reveal sit along the bottom, with social-media UI snippets overlaid.

idea.rationale: The content vehicle is a serialised three-part teaser delivered through a ritual already built into tennis broadcasts: instead of his usual signature on the camera lens after a win, Carlos Alcaraz wrote three cryptic messages ('No me lo creo, no more excuses', 'TUZUH? Hoy 8…', 'No more secrets') across consecutive Indian Wells matches, with the OOH and social reveal closing the loop on a new Netflix series. Treating the camera-lens signature as a serialised content channel is original within sports broadcasting, though the artefact itself is short-form teasers rather than a stand-alone entertainment piece.

strategy.rationale: Netflix is the global streaming platform whose sports-documentary slate (Drive to Survive, Break Point, Full Swing, Quarterback) makes athlete-led launches part of its product roadmap, so an Alcaraz-led tease for a tennis title sits inside the company's normal go-to-market. The context (Alcaraz at Indian Wells 2024, a documented top-card tournament moment) is verifiable. No richer audience insight is named on the board beyond the broadcast-hijack opportunity itself, which keeps Strategy modest.

execution.rationale: Orchestration uses tennis broadcast rights (40+ channels carrying Indian Wells) as an effectively free, global earned-media surface, with Times Square OOH and social as the reveal channels. The cryptic-to-clear sequence over three matches is the structural device that turns one signature into a campaign. The native check is tight, though: there is no stand-alone entertainment artefact the audience would seek out independently — just a series of broadcast moments that points at a trailer.

impact.rationale: The +20M audience and +700M earned reach across 160 countries are entrant-reported reach figures derived largely from the underlying broadcast audience that Netflix did not pay for — credible as a sponsorship-amplification proxy, but not a Netflix-relevant business outcome. The board shows zero data on the actual outcome a streamer needs (sign-ups, viewership of the series being launched, completion rate), which is the conspicuous absence. Press breadth (CNN, Forbes, The Guardian, Hollywood Reporter, ESPN, El País, Cosmopolitan) is a credible earned signal.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because hijacking the camera-lens signature ritual is genuinely clever, but it is teaser media riding on free broadcast reach — and the streaming business outcome (viewership lift for the series) is conspicuously missing.

The context is directly verifiable: the CDC has reported firearms as the leading cause of death for US children and teens since 2020, and the chart appears on the board. The use of pediatric-cancer iconography is justified by the comparative-mortality data, not a rhetorical flourish.

execution.rationale: Craft is exceptionally high for a nonprofit budget: Jose Padilha (Narcos, Robocop) directs, James Valentine of Maroon 5 scores, BUF (Avatar, Batman Begins) handles VFX, and the film is amplified through a coalition of celebrity endorsers (Common, James Valentine, Emily Wickersham, Shepard Fairey 'Obey Giant', Jesse Royal). The distribution mix — film-quality cinema with social-driven influencer push and earned press — is exactly tuned to a legislative-advocacy audience of policymakers and primetime news producers.

impact.rationale: The board carries no hard metric block, which is unusual for Entertainment Gold, but it does name a concrete, jury-credible business outcome: Politico is credited with attributing the campaign as a contributing force behind the Biden administration's closing of the 'gun-show loophole' and the expansion of background checks — a measurable legislative result is the right outcome for an advocacy NGO and far rarer than reach figures. Earned coverage across CNN, The Times, Forbes, The Guardian, ESPN, El País, Hollywood Reporter, Telemundo and Politico is breadth-credible. The lack of any reach / completion-rate figure on screen is a soft spot.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Gold because the bait-and-switch only earns the right to exist when the comparative CDC data actually backs it, and the legislative outcome is a real business result for an advocacy NGO — not a reach number.

The keynote-film-recontextualisation — opening a product-launch event with no product, only the people the product kept alive — is a clean idea but a familiar move in Apple's own brand-film lineage.

Replacing the gadget-spec film with a survivor documentary is direct mission-fit for a company already positioning itself around 'products that enrich life'. There is no external data point cited on the board — the case rests on the verifiable existence of the features and the survivors, which is enough.

execution.rationale: Craft is Apple-grade: documentary-format interviews with hundreds of real survivors, no script, no recreation, premiered as the September event opener with full live broadcast surround. Distribution is limited to the keynote stream and post-event social cut-downs — there is no festival circuit, no broader theatrical or streaming release, which keeps the orchestration narrow for an Entertainment entry. The native-content check is satisfied (it is a stand-alone documentary), but the audience is largely a captive keynote viewership rather than an opt-in one.

impact.rationale: 29.8M live viewers is the right reach number for a keynote curtain-raiser, but it is the underlying keynote audience Apple commands every September — the film did not earn its audience, it inherited it. There is no quantified lift in Apple Watch sales, Emergency SOS activations, or category awareness for the safety features attributable to the film, which is the conspicuous absence for an entry that frames itself around behaviour change. Press from Forbes, Inc. and others is favourable but qualitative.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because the survivor documentary is craft-credible and on-mission, but the audience was the captive keynote crowd and the impact case has no business-outcome anchor beyond the inherited viewership.

A blind broadcaster commentates an NBA Playoff game, live.
Mechanisms: purpose-driven-product, accessibility-tech-platform, live-broadcast-takeover, branded-franchise, talent-as-platform
Idea: Meet Cameron Black. Born blind. With a big dream. Cameron Black was born completely blind and always wanted to be a sports broadcaster. Together with Cameron, Michelob ULTRA combined and evolved different sets of technologies, creating a new multisensorial language, allowing him to understand the game of basketball so comprehensively that he was able to commentate it. On April 21, 2023, Cameron Black commentated Game 3 of the NBA Playoffs as the New York Knicks faced down the Cleveland Cavaliers in front of millions of fans. His commentary was seamlessly integrated into the broadcast as play-by-play live during the game, color commentary during play breaks, and as a full 2.5-hour live stream online.
Insight: Blind sports fans are excluded from basketball because the sport is too fast and fluid for traditional audio description — give a blind broadcaster the tools and he can call the game himself.

idea.rationale: The content vehicle is a live NBA Playoff broadcast — Knicks vs Cavaliers, Game 3, April 21 2023 — in which Cameron Black, a blind sports broadcaster, called play-by-play in real time using a custom multisensorial system (AURA: haptic feedback, spatial audio, refreshable Braille with generative AI, gesture control). Letting a blind person commentate a live NBA Playoff game is a structurally original entertainment moment because the talent and the assistive-tech platform are inseparable from the broadcast itself.

strategy.rationale: Michelob ULTRA is AB InBev's low-calorie beer brand whose 'It's Only Worth It If You Enjoy It' platform anchors a multi-year NBA sponsorship as the league's official beer partner — making an accessibility play inside the NBA broadcast is direct sponsorship-asset extension, not borrowed purpose. The board cites 284M blind and visually impaired people worldwide, which aligns with WHO public figures, and the insight that basketball is uniquely hard to follow without sight is operationally credible. The 'we wanted everyone to enjoy the game' line is a coherent extension of the existing brand platform.

execution.rationale: Orchestration is dense: pre-game, play-by-play, behind-the-tech segment, Q1 in-depth commentary, Madison Square Garden jumbotron appearance, Q4 commentary, legacy announcement, plus a 2.5-hour live-stream of the full call. The AURA stack (haptic vest, spatial audio, refreshable Braille, gesture control, gen-AI summarisation) is real custom engineering rather than off-the-shelf assistive tech, and the in-arena moment plus on-air integration with the broadcaster makes the platform credible. The promised October 2023 consumer app launch (named on the board) is the scaling layer.

impact.rationale: 21,000 in-arena, 10.4M live-TV viewers and 12.1M social-media viewers are the audience pickup for the NBA broadcast Michelob ULTRA had already paid sponsorship for — credible reach but partially inherited. Press recognition from NYT ('Changing the Game'), USA Today, The Athletic and the NBA itself is unpaid and on-topic. The business outcome a beer brand would care about (sales lift, share gain in the NBA-watching beer market, accessibility-app downloads after launch) is not quantified on the board, so the case rests on reach plus the existence of the technology rather than on outcome data.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because the live-broadcast accessibility moment is genuinely first-of-kind and the AURA tech is real custom engineering, but the impact case rests on inherited NBA reach with no anchored business outcome for Michelob ULTRA.

campaign: The Time Travel Vessel Which Skipped Valentine's Day
one_liner: A boat that skips Valentine's Day in one minute.
search_doc:
A boat that skips Valentine's Day in one minute.
Mechanisms: live-stream-stunt, calendar-jailbreak, branded-experiment-film, anti-holiday-positioning
Idea: We performed a 'time travel' experiment where 3 passengers who hate Valentine's Day successfully skipped the entire day, in just one minute. How? They boarded a vessel which crossed the International Date Line between American Samoa and Samoa at exactly 11:59 PM on 13 February 2024 (American Samoa time), thereby skipping directly to 12:00 AM on 15 February — the local time on the other side. The entire operation was live-streamed to millions of viewers, and the campaign rapidly became the hottest topic across social media platforms, reaching over 230 million users, with 1 million+ users reposting the trailer.
Insight: Some haters of Valentine's Day would literally cross an ocean to avoid the date — so we let them.

The calendar-jailbreak premise is genuinely original because the laws-of-time-zones loophole makes it physically literal rather than metaphorical, and the stunt itself is the content.

strategy.rationale: Cadbury 5 Star is Mondelez's mid-tier India chocolate brand whose long-running 'Eat 5 Star, Do Nothing' platform — anchored by the cult Ramesh-and-Suresh comic universe — explicitly positions the bar as the anti-effort, anti-occasion snack, so a stunt that lets people skip a forced-romance occasion is exact platform extension rather than borrowed purpose. The cultural insight that a sizeable cohort actively dislikes Valentine's Day is broadly observable, though no consumer-research data point is cited on the board to size it.

execution.rationale: Logistics are non-trivial: chartering a vessel, sourcing three real participants, coordinating a live stream across the date line at a precise minute, and producing a trailer to seed the moment beforehand. The single-event timing creates a genuine 'tune in or miss it' broadcast hook, with the trailer reposted across social driving most of the share volume. There is no creator-collaboration or earned-press partnership layer named on the board beyond organic reposts, which keeps orchestration relatively thin.

impact.rationale: 1.3B impressions, 230M views, 2.2M interactions and $8M earned PR are all entrant-reported reach figures — credible scale, but reach proxies rather than business outcomes. No category-relevant outcome (5 Star sales lift, share change in the India chocolate category over the Valentine's window, brand-platform preference scores) is shown on the board, which is conspicuous for a Mondelez submission. No specific press logos are listed in coverage, which makes the breadth claim harder to assess.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because the date-line stunt makes the 'do nothing' platform physically literal in a way that earns press attention, but the impact case is impressions-only with no India-market sales or share outcome anchored to the moment.

Idea: To raise awareness and bring visibility to the Yanomami fight against illegal gold cartels, we partnered with Salgueiro, a Brazilian Samba School that crashed the biggest spectacle on the planet — the Rio de Janeiro Carnival. It became the perfect stage to forward the message to a global audience and a new front of what is happening to the Yanomami.
Insight: The Yanomami's slow-motion genocide is invisible to Brazilian audiences — but Carnival, the country's most-watched spectacle, can be hijacked as a global stage for an Indigenous rallying cry.

extracted.context: The Yanomami people are an Indigenous group that lives isolated from the main Brazilian population. We spent a month leading up to the game spreading a conspiracy theory that CeraVe was developed by a celebrity: actor Michael Cera. Only problem: Michael Cera isn't on social media. How could we get this all over the internet, when our celebrity had no presence online? We leaned into a robust PR, social, and influence play: 800+ publications from Daily Mail to New York Times. Partnering with meme accounts, podcasters, skinfluencers, you name it… The more CeraVe used their channels to deny it, the more people were invested. We put out all the information. Then let the internet, internet. People didn't just watch, they participated (in a conspiracy about a skincare brand!). 'Michael CeraVe' was everywhere. It became a global hot-topic debate which led to 9 Billion impressions BEFORE the commercial even aired. We revealed the truth during the most ever watched Super Bowl. Our commercial wasn't the hero like most Super Bowl spots. It was the conclusion to a month-long, fully immersive campaign. Comedy legends Tim & Eric directed a bizarre, hilarious, fever dream which was hailed as 'Perfect' (TODAY). In the spot, dermatologists reject Michael making it clear once and for all: CeraVe is developed with dermatologists. Not Michael Cera.
Insight: If you can't put a celebrity on the Super Bowl, plant a celebrity-shaped conspiracy and let the internet broadcast it for you.

impact.rationale: The board carries no metrics and no press-coverage entries, which is a significant rigour issue. Idea: To combat the gender bias, we decided to show how exciting women's football actually is. We created a compilation of unseen highlights of the French men's football team, showcasing powerful shots, amazing dribbles… only sensational actions. But the viewer didn't know he was actually watching women's football: we swapped the women's French team players with the men's starts from the French team, to make the audience watch their performances with fresh eyes.
Insight: Audiences who claim to find women's football less exciting are actually mis-attributing prejudice to skill — show them the same plays without the gender cue and they react like fans.

The reveal makes the viewer's own bias the punchline of the film, which is a structurally novel way to demonstrate skill parity rather than argue for it.

The context is verifiable: weeks before the 2023 Women's World Cup, no French broadcaster had secured TV rights — a documented public controversy in French sports media. The insight that audiences mis-attribute prejudice to skill is plausible and the film itself functions as the experimental proof rather than a claim.

execution.rationale: The deepfake-face-swap pipeline is technically demanding and the talent rights for using the men's stars' faces are non-trivial to clear. Phased release is calibrated to the prank: influencers first posted the 'unseen men's highlights' on Twitter, then revealed the trick a few hours later when engagement peaked, then the full film rolled across Orange's social estate and earned press worldwide within a day. The two-part reveal is what creates the share-driver, not the film alone.

impact.rationale: The headline +2B impressions, +200M organic views, +50M likes and PR coverage in 91 countries are entrant-reported and unaudited reach metrics — large but standard claims. The harder-to-fake signals are TIME ('Women's soccer can be just as exciting as men's') and ESPN ('A genius commercial') quotes, plus the fact that the film coincided with the broadcasting-rights deal eventually being secured in France. The business outcome a sponsor actually cares about — sustained TV ratings for the women's tournament — is not quantified on the board, which is the main soft spot.

why_it_won.verdict: Wins Gold because the face-swap is not a tech demo but the only honest way to prove the insight: viewers cheering 'the men's plays' were cheering the women all along.
